1: Psalm 87 is among the Psalter's most remarkable theological poems — a vision of Zion as the universal birthplace of all nations. Its brevity (seven verses) belies its theological density. "His foundation is on the holy mountains" — yĕsûdātô bĕharĕrê-qōdeš. The divine founding (yāsad) of Zion is a theological claim: YHWH himself established the city as his dwelling place. "Holy mountains" (plural) may refer to the two peaks of the Jerusalem mountain complex — Zion proper and Moriah (the temple mount) — or may be a poetic plural for the sacred mountain as a whole.
2: "The LORD loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwelling places of Jacob" — a direct preferential statement: YHWH has chosen Jerusalem above all Israel's sacred sites. "Gates of Zion" — city gates were not merely architectural features but sites of legal proceedings, commerce, and civic life (Ruth 4:1–11; Proverbs 31:23; Amos 5:10–12). "Dwelling places of Jacob (miškĕnôt yaʿăqōb)" — the tent-shrines and sanctuaries distributed throughout Israel: Shiloh, Bethel, Gilgal, Beersheba. YHWH's exclusive preference for Jerusalem underlies the Deuteronomic centralization of worship (Deuteronomy 12:5, 11; Psalms 16:2, 6, 11).
3: "Glorious things are spoken of you, O city of God" — nĕkabbādôt mĕdubbār bāk ʿîr hāʾĕlōhîm. "Glorious things (nĕkabbādôt)" — things of weight, substance, honor. "City of God (ʿîr hāʾĕlōhîm)" — a title for Jerusalem marking it as YHWH's own city (Psalms 46:4; Psalms 48:1, 8; Isaiah 60:14; Revelation 3:12). The Selah marks a pause before the extraordinary content of Psalms 87:4.
4: "Among those who acknowledge me I will name Rahab and Babylon; and behold — Philistia, Tyre, and Ethiopia: 'This one was born there'" — one of the most astonishing verses in the OT. Five nations are named — Israel's historic enemies: Rahab (the poetic name for Egypt, from the sea-monster image applied to Pharaoh in Isaiah 30:7; Psalms 51:9 — distinct from Rahab the chaos-dragon of Psalms 89:10 and Job 9:13), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush/Ethiopia. YHWH declares he will "name (ʾazkîr)" each as "born there (in Zion)" — former enemies become registered citizens of the city of God.
5: "Of Zion it will be said, 'This one and that one were born in her,' and the Most High himself will establish her" — the repeated "born there/in her" is the psalm's refrain of astonishment. The language of being "born in" a city was the standard citizenship language of the ancient world. The psalm claims that YHWH will register foreign nations as Zion-born citizens — a spiritual re-nativity that Paul takes up in Galatians 4:26 ("the Jerusalem above is our mother") and Hebrews 12:22 ("you have come to Mount Zion, the city of the living God"). "The Most High himself (wĕhûʾ yĕkônĕnehā ʿelyôn)" — it is divine action, not human initiative, that brings this about.
6: "When the LORD records the enrollment of the peoples, he will write, 'This one was born there'" — biktōb ʿammîm (in the writing/enrolling of peoples). The image is of a census or civic population register — the official record determining citizenship. In the ancient world, birth registers determined civic rights and identity; YHWH as divine scribe of nations keeps the ultimate register. Each nation is enrolled not by birth but by divine declaration. This divine enrollment anticipates Paul's concept of citizenship in the heavenly city (Philippians 3:20) and the "book of life" in which the redeemed are registered (Revelation 13:8; Psalms 20:15; Psalms 21:27).
7: "Singers and dancers alike say, 'All my springs of life are in you'" — wĕkol maʿăyānay bāk (and all my springs are in you). The psalm closes with liturgical response from those who celebrate: singers and dancers together declare that Zion is the source of all their life-springs. "Springs (maʿăyānîm)" — the spring/fountain imagery appears throughout the Psalter for YHWH as the source of life (Psalms 36:9: "with you is the fountain of life"; Jeremiah 2:13: YHWH as the spring of living water). In the ancient Near East, springs were sacred — the gift of life in a dry land. All life-sources of these worshippers, including the enrolled nations, flow from this city.
